questions.question: Explain the difference between a half adder and a full adder with truth tables.
keyPoints: Half adder has 2 inputs (A, B), full adder has 3 inputs (A, B, Cin)
Half adder produces SUM and CARRY outputs
Full adder handles carry from previous stage
Truth tables differ: half adder ignores input carry
Full adder is cascade of two half adders plus OR gate for carry

questions.question: Describe the concept of select lines in ALU design. How many select lines are needed for 12 operations?
keyPoints: Select lines determine which operation the ALU performs
Number of select lines = ceiling(log2 of number of operations)
For 12 operations: 2^4 = 16 >= 12, so 4 select lines needed
Select lines control multiplexers to choose correct result
More select lines allow more operations but increase complexity

questions.question: What are the various status flags in ALU? Explain any three flags.
keyPoints: Status flags indicate special conditions after ALU operation
ZERO FLAG: set when result is all zeros
CARRY FLAG: set when unsigned overflow occurs
OVERFLOW FLAG: set when signed overflow occurs
SIGN FLAG: reflects MSB of result (negative indicator)
PARITY FLAG: indicates odd/even number of 1s in result

questions.question: Explain how subtraction is performed in ALU using adders.
keyPoints: ALU uses two's complement method for subtraction
First compute one's complement (invert all bits of subtrahend)
Add 1 to get two's complement
Add this to the minuend using regular adder circuit
This eliminates need for separate subtractor hardware
Same adder circuit handles both addition and subtraction

questions.question: What is a multiplexer and how is it used in ALU design?
keyPoints: Multiplexer selects one input from multiple inputs based on select lines
Used in ALU to select between different operation results
2^n inputs require n select lines
Each operation result feeds into MUX inputs
Select lines choose which result appears at output
Enables single ALU to perform multiple functions

questions.question: Explain the working of a ripple carry adder with a diagram description.
keyPoints: Constructed by cascading N full adders for N-bit addition
Each full adder handles one bit position
Carry output of each stage becomes carry input of next
Propagation delay is main disadvantage (O(N))
Simple design but slow for large bit widths
Example: 4-bit adder needs 4 full adders connected in series

questions.question: Design a 4-bit ALU that can perform four arithmetic operations (ADD, SUBTRACT, INCREMENT, DECREMENT) and four logical operations (AND, OR, XOR, NOT). Show the select line configuration and explain the operation selection mechanism.
keyPoints: Need 3 select lines for 8 operations (2^3 = 8)
Define select code for each operation: S2S1S0
Arithmetic operations: 000=ADD, 001=SUB, 010=INC, 011=DEC
Logical operations: 100=AND, 101=OR, 110=XOR, 111=NOT
Use multiplexers to select between arithmetic and logical results
Arithmetic unit uses adder with MUX for operation selection
Logic unit uses separate logic gates for each operation
Final MUX selects between arithmetic and logical result based on S2
Carry flag for arithmetic, Zero flag for both
Control unit generates select codes based on instruction

questions.question: Compare ripple carry adder and carry look-ahead adder. Explain how carry look-ahead adder achieves faster computation.
keyPoints: Ripple carry adder: simple cascade of full adders, O(N) propagation delay
Carry look-ahead: computes carries in parallel, O(1) gate delay for carry generation
Generate function Gi = Ai AND Bi (carry produced at stage i)
Propagate function Pi = Ai XOR Bi (carry propagates through stage i)
Carry out Ci+1 = Gi OR (Pi AND Ci)
Look-ahead unit computes all carries simultaneously using these formulas
Trade-off: more complex circuitry but faster operation
For large bit widths, CLA is significantly faster
Cost: More gates required for CLA implementation
Used in modern processors for critical path optimization

questions.question: Explain the complete data flow in ALU during execution of an arithmetic instruction. Include the role of registers, control signals, and status flags.
keyPoints: Control unit decodes instruction and generates control signals
Operands loaded from registers into ALU input latches
Operation select lines set based on instruction opcode
Input data paths enabled by control signals
ALU performs designated operation on operands
Result stored in destination register
Status flags updated based on result
Zero flag: check if result equals zero
Carry flag: check for unsigned overflow
Overflow flag: check for signed overflow
Sign flag: reflects MSB of result
Flag values stored in PSW (Program Status Word)
Next instruction uses these flags for conditional operations
